steps.title: Identify the general-to-specific relationship
steps.content: The first sentence makes a general claim — the station "does much to serve the local Dakota-speaking community." The second sentence provides specific details of how: it "broadcasts local news and music as well as programming in the Dakota language." The transition must signal this shift from a general statement to concrete examples.

steps.title: Select the transition for exemplification
steps.content:
The logical relationship between the two sentences is specification. Sentence 1 makes a broad claim; Sentence 2 gives the concrete evidence. The blank needs a transition signaling that the second sentence provides particulars supporting the general claim.
  • Choice A — "Instead,": signals a replacement or correction — but Sentence 2 is not replacing Sentence 1 with something different. ✗
  • Choice B — "Specifically,": signals that Sentence 2 provides concrete details supporting the general claim in Sentence 1. ✓
  • Choice C — "As a result,": signals cause-and-effect — Sentence 2 is not an outcome of Sentence 1; it illustrates it. ✗
  • Choice D — "Nevertheless,": signals contrast despite an obstacle — Sentence 2 is fully consistent with, not contrary to, Sentence 1. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
